Atlanta Restaurant Openings
Close Company Bar Debuts in Atlanta, Joining Acclaimed Death & Co Universe
Opening next week in Old Fourth Ward, Close Company Atlanta features lemon pepper pockets, $9 cocktails, and chill vibes
by
Colleen McNally Arnett
Oct 29, 2025, 2:31 PM UTC
Facebook
Link
Image:
Luke Beard
Facebook
Link
“It’s been a wild year,” says David Kaplan, CEO of
Gin & Luck
, the overarching hospitality group leading the storied Death & Co empire. It’s early morning where he resides in Seattle, and the countdown is on until his latest project, the Atlanta iteration of
Close Company
, opens on
November 6
.
For his group, the last six months alone have included introducing the
Municipal Grand — the first hotel from his Midnight Auteur firm in Savannah, Georgia
, and
its basement lounge, The Hot Eye
, as well as launching the first neon-lit location of
Close Company in Nashville’s Germantown
, quickly followed by a second one inside Las Vegas’ The Venetian Resort.
Where Death & Co is known for its impressive awards, dimly lit interiors, and dapperly dressed bartenders, Close Company celebrates a more casual, funky approach to the neighborhood bar. Checkered floors and diner-esque elements nod to timeless, Americana dives, albeit reimagined in a modern way, with art curated by Field Trip Art Advisory.
Close Company celebrates a more casual, funky approach to the neighborhood bar.
Luke Beard
“It’s the unbuttoned, unpretentious younger sister of Death & Co: a little louder, a little looser, and unapologetically fun,” reads the
Close Company website
. “A bit of grit, a lot of soul, and no bullshit.”
That means removing, or at least simplifying, some of the barriers to entry and decision points for guests seeking a fun night out by prioritizing spontaneity over pre-planning and commitment required to secure reservations.
“‘No bullshit’ is our own way of teasing ourselves, really,” Kaplan says. “We really just want [Close Company] to be as easy and welcoming as possible.”
Close Company operates on a first-come, first-served basis, offering various seating options and standing areas. In place of clever, provocative drink names (i.e., the iconic
Naked & Famous
), Close Company’s menu is more straightforward, with naming conventions that describe ingredients and what they offer, such as the Coconut Highball made with Dewar’s Scotch and coconut soda for $15.
Espresso martini and tonic at Close Company Bar.
Luke Beard
The cocktails, including zero-proof options, range from $9 for a Ranch Water (Vichy Catalán, tequila, and lime) to $17 for the Champagne Lassi (Banhez Destilado de Mango, yogurt, fancy bubbles). “The price point is far lower than Death & Co based upon all of the various efficiencies that we build in and the kind of tech that goes into it to make it really fast and efficient to execute,” Kaplan explains.
While Atlanta’s location is the third to deliver, it was actually the first in the pipeline, though finding the right address required careful consideration. When Kaplan and his partner, Alex Day, and former partner Devon Tarby, connected with Jim Irwin, president of New City, to tour the Beltline-facing Old Fourth Ward office development, everything clicked into place.
“We’re on the ground floor of one of the Darth Vader-looking, Olson Kundig office buildings and even though they’re kind of modern, Gothic, and absolutely beautiful, it’s a departure for us,” Kaplan explains. “But one that we felt that the Close Company brand really fits nicely into and adapts well.” He adds that it harkens to the Normandie Club in LA — a former project under the Proprietors LLC hospitality firm that predated Gin & Luck.
Close Company Atlanta’s nearby attractions, such as FOURTH Hotel and Ponce City Market, along the Beltline’s well-trafficked Eastside Trail, also play into the vision for Close Company as an easy, approachable bar where locals become regulars.
“The Beltline itself is just an incredible thing that I wish every city had. It really makes a neighborhood feel timeless and very anchored,” Kaplan says. “It feels like it is built in such a thoughtful way to really stand the test of time.”
Lemon Pepper Chicken pastry pocket is served as a bar snack at Close Company.
Luke Beard
Luke Beard
Just as the Beltline brought more Atlantans out of their cars and onto its public multi-use greenway, Close Company is designed to encourage guests to move and mingle, like a game of musical chairs. Bar snacks, such as the Atlanta-inspired Lemon Pepper Chicken pastry pocket, are self-served from an automat-inspired snack window using custom buzzers.
He adds that the name “Close Company” also alludes to the familial network of brands that have grown out of Death & Co since
the original location
first electrified New York’s East Village on New Year’s Eve 2006, as well as previous collaborators — such as Natasha David of
Nitecap
— who serve as inspiration for the new concept.
Close Company will feature several small bar snacks and reasonably-priced cocktails like a Ranch Water for $9.
Luke Beard
Part of Kaplan’s motivation to further develop the Death & Co ecosystem is to provide more opportunities for team members to advance alongside it. “We’re now at a point in our growth and with such phenomenal tenured employees that we’re putting people into positions of brand ownership,” he says, noting that Close Company reflects the decade-plus work of bartender Lauren Corriveau and beverage director Matthew Belanger.
Looking ahead, Midnight Auteur plans to open more hotels in Boulder, Colorado, and Bozeman, Montana. Meanwhile, Death & Co, which already boasts a presence in New York City, Denver, Los Angeles, and Washington, D.C., will soon add Seattle and expand internationally, with two outposts slated for Australia.
“It’s always kind of been a crazy world out there, and so to create spaces and experiences where people can come together and really be in the moment, I think that has always been needed. I think it will always be needed.”
